findings.title: Search Relevance Score Drop
findings.description: The recent deployment (v2.4.1) has caused a significant drop in search relevance scores, which could lead to decreased user satisfaction and increased support volume. This is a critical issue that needs immediate attention.
assumptions: The drop in relevance is directly related to the new ranking algorithm introduced in v2.4.1.

evidence.excerpt: Search relevance score dropped 15% after deploy v2.4.1

recommendations: Conduct a root-cause analysis of the ranking algorithm.
Consider a rollback if no quick fix is found.

findings.title: Feature Flag Dependency for Rollback
findings.description: The ability to rollback the v2.4.1 deployment is blocked by a dependent feature flag change. This needs to be addressed to allow for quick remediation of the relevance score drop.
assumptions: The feature flag change is the only barrier to rollback.

evidence.excerpt: Rollback was considered but blocked by a dependent feature flag change.

recommendations: Identify and resolve the feature flag dependency.

findings.title: Increased Support Volume
findings.description: The spike in support volume due to irrelevant search results indicates a significant user experience issue. This could lead to further dissatisfaction and churn if not addressed promptly.
assumptions: The increase in support volume is directly correlated with the changes made in v2.4.1.

evidence.excerpt: Support volume has spiked 3x since v2.4.1 rolled out.

recommendations: Prioritize fixing the ranking algorithm to improve relevance.
Monitor support tickets closely for trends.

findings.title: Search Latency Issue on Mobile
findings.description: The increased search latency on mobile web indicates a potential performance issue with the new ranking model. This needs to be profiled and optimized to prevent SLO breaches.
assumptions: The new ranking model is causing the increased latency.

evidence.excerpt: P95 search latency on mobile web has climbed to 890ms.

recommendations: Profile the new ranking model and optimize for mobile.

findings.title: Missing Analytics Events for New Query Types
findings.description: The lack of analytics events for image and voice search means there is no visibility into their performance. This is critical for measuring success and making informed decisions.
assumptions: Analytics events can be added without major changes to the existing pipeline.

evidence.excerpt: No analytics events were instrumented for these new query types.

recommendations: Implement baseline instrumentation for new query types.

findings.title: Establish Baseline Metrics for Image Search
findings.description: Without baseline metrics for image search, it is impossible to measure success or define criteria for future improvements. Establishing these metrics is essential.
assumptions: Baseline metrics can be established with existing data collection methods.

evidence.excerpt: We have no baseline metrics to measure against.

recommendations: Define and implement baseline metrics for image search.

findings.title: Fix Autocomplete Cache Configuration
findings.description: The stale data in autocomplete suggestions is compounding the relevance problem. The cache TTL needs to be reverted to the original setting to improve user experience.
assumptions: Reverting the cache TTL will resolve the stale data issue.

evidence.excerpt: Cache TTL was inadvertently extended to 7 days.

recommendations: Revert the cache TTL to 24 hours.

summary: The recent deployment of the SearchBoost Query Engine has led to significant issues, including a drop in search relevance scores and increased support volume. Immediate actions are required to address these critical risks, particularly focusing on the ranking algorithm, feature flag dependencies, and performance issues on mobile. A phased delivery plan is proposed to prioritize the most impactful fixes in the MVP phase.
